questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman complains of gradually worsening vision over the past year, especially difficulty recognising faces and reading. She has no pain. On examination, visual acuity is 6/18 in the right eye and 6/12 in the left. Fundoscopy reveals multiple small, yellowish deposits in the macula and a few areas of retinal pigment epithelium atrophy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of drusen and RPE atrophy without exudation is diagnostic of dry AMD. Wet AMD would show signs of fluid or haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man reports needing brighter light to read and difficulty adapting to dim lighting. His visual acuity is 6/9 in both eyes. Fundoscopy shows a few drusen but no neovascular changes. Amsler grid testing reveals subtle metamorphopsia. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Early dry AMD presents with drusen and subtle metamorphopsia. PVD typically causes floaters and flashes, not gradual visual decline with drusen.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with sudden distortion and a dark spot in the centre of her vision in the left eye for 2 days. She has known early dry AMD in the right eye. Visual acuity is 6/60 in the left eye. Fundoscopy reveals a greyish-green lesion with subretinal fluid and haemorrhage at the macula. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Sudden onset of distortion and central scotoma with subretinal fluid and haemorrhage is classic for wet AMD. Retinal detachment would show a retinal tear and detached retina.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with known wet AMD in one eye treated with anti-VEGF injections presents with sudden, severe vision loss in the same eye. Fundoscopy shows a large, dark, dome-shaped elevation under the macula with overlying retinal vessels visible. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Submacular haemorrhage secondary to neovascular AMD
questions.explanation: A dark dome-shaped elevation with visible retinal vessels is typical of submacular haemorrhage. Choroidal melanoma would be a solid mass with overlying orange pigment.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman of South Asian origin presents with recurrent episodes of blurred vision and metamorphopsia. She has no other ocular history. Fundoscopy shows multiple serous detachments of the retinal pigment epithelium and a reddish-orange nodule at the macula. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (a subtype of neovascular AMD)
questions.explanation: PCV is a variant of neovascular AMD characterised by polypoidal lesions and serous RPE detachments, more common in younger patients of Asian descent. Central serous chorioretinopathy typically has a single serous retinal detachment.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with gradual, painless central vision loss in the right eye over several months. On fundoscopy, there are multiple drusen and areas of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) change in the macula. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of early age-related macular degeneration (AMD)?
questions.explanation: OCT is the gold standard for diagnosing early AMD as it provides cross-sectional images of the retina, clearly showing drusen, RPE changes, and photoreceptor integrity. FFA is reserved for suspected wet AMD; Amsler grid is for self-monitoring; perimetry is not diagnostic; fundus photography is adjunctive.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man presents with sudden onset of metamorphopsia and a central scotoma in the left eye. Visual acuity is 6/60. Fundoscopy reveals a yellowish-grey lesion under the fovea. Which investigation is the single most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis of wet age-related macular degeneration (AMD)?
questions.explanation: FFA is the definitive test for diagnosing choroidal neovascularization (CNV) in wet AMD, demonstrating early hyperfluorescence and late leakage. OCT can show fluid but FFA confirms active CNV and guides treatment. FAF is for geographic atrophy; Amsler grid is screening; B-scan is for dense media opacities.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old asymptomatic woman is found to have soft drusen and RPE hyperpigmentation in both maculae during a routine eye examination. She has no visual symptoms. Which investigation is most appropriate for risk stratification of progression to advanced AMD?
options: Genetic testing for complement factor H polymorphisms
questions.explanation: OCT is the best tool for risk stratification in early AMD, as it quantifies drusen volume and detects subclinical RPE and photoreceptor changes. FFA is not indicated without symptoms; genetic testing is not routinely recommended for risk stratification; ERG is for generalised retinal dysfunction.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old patient with known dry AMD in both eyes reports a sudden worsening of central vision in the right eye over two days. Visual acuity has dropped from 6/12 to 6/36. Which investigation is most appropriate to rule out conversion to wet AMD?
questions.explanation: OCT is the first-line test to detect subretinal or intraretinal fluid, indicating active neovascularisation. It is quick, non-invasive, and highly sensitive. FFA may follow if OCT is inconclusive, but OCT is the initial best test. FAF assesses geographic atrophy; Amsler grid is subjective; perimetry is not diagnostic for wet AMD.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old patient with known age-related macular degeneration undergoes optical coherence tomography (OCT). The scan shows subretinal hyperreflective material and intraretinal cysts in the foveal region. What is the most likely diagnosis based on these OCT findings?
questions.explanation: Subretinal hyperreflective material (correlating with CNV or fibrous tissue) and intraretinal cysts (due to fluid) are classic OCT features of active wet AMD. Geographic atrophy shows thinning and RPE loss without fluid; macular hole has full-thickness defect; epiretinal membrane has wrinkling; central serous retinopathy shows serous detachment without hyperreflective material.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset central scotoma and metamorphopsia in her right eye. OCT confirms subfoveal neovascular AMD with a small pigment epithelial detachment. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab or aflibercept) as first-line for active neovascular AMD. Photodynamic therapy is reserved for specific subtypes where anti-VEGF is not suitable.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with bilateral intermediate dry AMD (large drusen and pigmentary changes) and visual acuity 6/12 in both eyes asks about preventative therapy. Which intervention is most appropriate to reduce the risk of progression to advanced AMD?
options: Oral vitamin C and E alone
Oral lutein and zeaxanthin with zinc and copper (AREDS2)
questions.explanation: The AREDS2 trial demonstrated that a formulation containing lutein, zeaxanthin, zinc, and copper reduces the risk of progression in intermediate AMD. Anti-VEGF is not indicated for dry AMD, and other supplements lack evidence.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known wet AMD on monthly ranibizumab presents with acute floaters and a large vitreous haemorrhage. B-scan shows no retinal tear and the haemorrhage obscures the macula. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: Vitreous haemorrhage in wet AMD typically originates from the CNV; anti-VEGF therapy should be continued. Urgent vitrectomy or tPA is not first-line, and switching agents is not indicated solely for this complication.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with wet AMD completed three monthly loading doses of ranibizumab with good anatomical and functional response. Which of the following long-term management strategies is contraindicated?
options: PRN (pro re nata) regimen with monthly monitoring
questions.explanation: NICE recommends treat-and-extend or PRN regimens with monitoring; observation without follow-up risks undetected recurrence and vision loss, making it contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old patient with wet AMD develops anterior uveitis after two consecutive ranibizumab injections, with no alternative cause found. What is the most appropriate next therapy?
questions.explanation: Aflibercept is a licensed alternative anti-VEGF with a different molecular structure, often used when inflammation recurs with ranibizumab. Topical steroids may be added but switching is preferred for recurrent uveitis.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents to the ED with sudden onset distorted vision and a central scotoma in her right eye for 2 days. She has a history of hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. On examination, visual acuity is 6/18 in the right eye, 6/6 in the left. Amsler grid testing reveals metamorphopsia and a central blur. Fundoscopy shows a greyish-green lesion at the macula with associated subretinal fluid. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Advise strict eye rest and review in 2 weeks
Prescribe high-dose oral vitamin C and E supplements
Arrange for a CT scan of the head to rule out stroke
Start oral acetazolamide 250 mg four times daily
Refer urgently to ophthalmology for same-day assessment and possible intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy
questions.explanation: The clinical picture is highly suggestive of neovascular (wet) age-related macular degeneration (AMD). NICE guidelines recommend urgent referral to ophthalmology (within 2 weeks, but same-day if acute vision loss) for assessment including macular OCT and potential intravitreal anti-VEGF treatment. Delaying treatment can worsen outcomes.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man attends his routine diabetic eye screening. He is asymptomatic. Fundoscopy reveals numerous large drusen (>125 µm) in both maculae, with no evidence of choroidal neovascularisation. Visual acuity is 6/9 in both eyes. He asks what he should do next. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to ophthalmology for photodynamic therapy
Arrange for a carotid Doppler ultrasound
Reassure and advise self-monitoring with an Amsler grid, with no treatment required
Prescribe a statin to reduce drusen progression
Start a daily supplement containing lutein and zeaxanthin
questions.explanation: This patient has dry AMD with large drusen but no visual symptoms and no advanced AMD in one eye. According to NICE, AREDS2 vitamin supplementation is only recommended for bilateral intermediate AMD or advanced AMD in one eye. The appropriate management is reassurance and advice to monitor symptoms using an Amsler grid, with no active treatment.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman presents to the ED with acute painless loss of central vision in her left eye that started 4 hours ago. She has known dry AMD in both eyes. On examination, visual acuity is hand movements in the left eye, 6/12 in the right. Fundoscopy of the left eye reveals a large submacular haemorrhage obscuring the fovea. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange same-day ophthalmology referral for urgent assessment and possible intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy
Perform a CT scan of the head
questions.explanation: Acute submacular haemorrhage from wet AMD is a time-critical emergency. The most appropriate action is immediate ophthalmology referral for evaluation and possible intravitreal anti-VEGF injection. Steroids and aspirin are not indicated, and a head CT is not necessary without neurological symptoms.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known neovascular AMD in his left eye, receiving monthly intravitreal ranibizumab, presents to the eye casualty with a 1-day history of red, painful left eye with blurred vision. On examination, visual acuity is light perception only. The left eye has conjunctival injection, anterior chamber cells, and a 1 mm hypopyon. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Add oral ciprofloxacin 750 mg twice daily
Schedule for a vitrectomy in 1 week
Refer for a vitreous tap and intravitreal antibiotics
questions.explanation: This is likely endophthalmitis following intravitreal injection, a sight-threatening emergency. The immediate step is to perform a vitreous tap and inject intravitreal antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin and ceftazidime). Topical steroids alone are insufficient. Systemic antibiotics are adjunctive.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a 2-year history of dry AMD (large drusen in both eyes, no neovascularisation) is awaiting cataract surgery for bilateral significant cataracts. She is otherwise systemically well. She asks about the risks of surgery. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Perform cataract surgery without any additional precautions
Refer to ophthalmology for prophylactic anti-VEGF injection before surgery
Start systemic doxycycline to reduce inflammation
Discuss that cataract surgery may increase the risk of progression to wet AMD and ensure close monitoring post-operatively
Cancel cataract surgery due to risk of progression of AMD
questions.explanation: In patients with dry AMD, cataract surgery may accelerate progression to neovascular AMD. The correct management is to discuss this risk with the patient, consider pre-operative OCT to rule out subclinical wet AMD, and arrange for close post-operative follow-up. Prophylactic anti-VEGF is not recommended by NICE.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of blurred central vision and distortion when reading. She has noticed a grey spot in the centre of her vision in the left eye. On examination, visual acuity is 6/9 right, 6/18 left, and Amsler grid shows distortion. Which single feature on ophthalmoscopy would be most concerning for neovascular AMD?
questions.explanation: Subretinal fluid indicates active choroidal neovascularisation (CNV) and is the hallmark of neovascular AMD, requiring urgent referral for anti-VEGF therapy. Geographic atrophy and drusen are features of dry AMD and do not require immediate referral.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old male with known drusen in both eyes presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset of severe pain and blurred vision in the left eye over the past 2 hours. He vomited once. On examination, visual acuity is hand movements, cornea is cloudy, and pupil is mid-dilated. Which finding is most critical in this patient?
questions.explanation: An intraocular pressure of 60 mmHg indicates acute angle closure glaucoma (ACAG), a medical emergency that can cause irreversible vision loss within hours. AMD does not cause pain, and other findings are less immediately sight-threatening.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with wet AMD treated with three monthly intravitreal bevacizumab injections presents 2 days after the last injection with increasing pain, redness, and blurred vision. On examination, visual acuity has dropped from 6/12 to counting fingers. Which clinical sign is most indicative of endophthalmitis?
questions.explanation: Hypopyon (pus in the anterior chamber) is a classic sign of infectious endophthalmitis, a sight-threatening emergency after intravitreal injection. Vitreous haze is also common but hypopyon is more specific and mandates immediate treatment.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old man with dry AMD and large drusen, who also has atrial fibrillation and is on warfarin (INR 2.3), reports sudden, painless severe vision loss in his right eye this morning. He now sees a large central shadow with no distortion. Which fundus finding is most likely to be seen in this emergency?
questions.explanation: Subretinal haemorrhage under the fovea is typical of massive submacular haemorrhage, a complication of wet AMD that can occur spontaneously or with anticoagulation. It causes sudden central vision loss and requires urgent ophthalmology referral for possible surgical evacuation or anti-VEGF therapy.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with wet AMD has been stable on a treat-and-extend regimen of ranibizumab every 12 weeks. At a routine follow-up, he is asymptomatic and visual acuity remains 6/12. However, his OCT reveals a new finding. Which OCT feature indicates active disease requiring prompt re-treatment?
questions.explanation: New subretinal fluid on OCT is the hallmark of active neovascular AMD and indicates recurrence of leakage from choroidal neovascularisation. This crosses the treatment threshold, even in the absence of symptoms, to prevent further vision loss.
